doom.driver: 'AI helped pick the first thousand targets in Iran'
doom.blurb: THE ALGORITHM LEARNED TO KILL FIRST
doom.summary: Buried between oil tankers on fire and gold hitting historic lows is a quietly civilizational headline: AI systems are now selecting airstrike targets in a live war. This isn't a think-piece about future risks — it's a dispatch from week five of an active conflict where the machine is already in the loop. Meanwhile Spain and Italy are shutting US bombers out of their bases, Zelensky is being asked to ease up on Russia because energy prices are already through the roof, and Brent crude is flirting with $117. The Iran war isn't just a Middle East story anymore — it's the load-bearing pillar of every other crisis on this list.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: AI reportedly selected the first thousand airstrike targets in the Iran war — autonomous targeting at scale in a live conflict is a threshold moment for AI-enabled warfare.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Spain and Italy are denying US military bases for Iran strikes, Zelensky is being pressured to reduce attacks on Russia, and Gulf stock markets have lost $120bn — the war is fragmenting Western alliances in real time.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: No international framework governs AI targeting in warfare; the Iran war is writing the precedent by default with zero democratic deliberation.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: The Axios NPM package was compromised to drop a remote access trojan — supply chain attacks on critical dev infrastructure are accelerating alongside AI deployment.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Food shock described as 'inevitable' due to the Iran war, with fertilizer and fuel prices already cascading — a humanitarian slow-burn crisis is layering onto the acute military one.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, ground-level view looking straight up from flat desert earth at Natanz enrichment facility, the low earthen berms and ventilation stacks towering overhead against a deep blue-black 3 AM sky, we are lying on the ochre dust looking upward at the massive underbelly of a B-2 Spirit stealth bomber passing silently overhead, its flying wing silhouette filling the upper frame, charcoal gray against charcoal sky, the subtle curve of its leading edge barely distinguishable from darkness, below it the ventilation stacks of the buried facility rise like black monoliths against a sky faintly backlit by distant firelight, a curved monitor array glows at the base of a nearby command bunker entrance visible at the frame's lower edge, dark-mode UI interface showing scrolling cyan text and numbered coordinate rows, color-coded red and amber threat tags blinking, the ground beneath us is baked pale desert, cracked salt flat surface rendered in precise pixel tiles, crimson light from somewhere beyond the frame reflects off the smooth underside of the bomber and the metallic ventilation infrastructure, oppressive scale contrast between tiny bunker entrance below and the vast aircraft above, deep crimson and charcoal palette throughout, high contrast, no antialiasing, 16-bit SNES sprite detail.

dawn.driver: Powerful cholesterol drug cuts heart attack risk by 31%.
dawn.blurb: HEART ATTACKS JUST GOT 31% LESS LIKELY
dawn.summary: Even as war, oil shocks, and geopolitical chaos dominate the headlines, scientists are quietly winning battles that matter most: the ones inside our bodies. Evolocumab, a cholesterol-lowering drug, has been shown to cut heart attack risk by 31% — a genuinely significant leap in cardiovascular medicine that will save real lives regardless of what happens in the Strait of Hormuz. Heart disease remains the world's leading killer, and a breakthrough of this magnitude is a reminder that human ingenuity keeps compounding even when the news cycle does not.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: A 31% reduction in heart attack risk is a landmark finding that could reshape prevention guidelines for millions of high-risk patients worldwide.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: The US reopened its embassy in Venezuela, a quiet diplomatic thaw that signals at least one frozen relationship is beginning to warm.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Gazan mothers reunited with children evacuated as premature newborns two years ago — a small, luminous act of reunion amid ongoing devastation.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: South Korea's $17 billion emergency budget prioritizes cushioning energy costs, with green energy transition cited as the long-term path forward.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Céline Dion announced comeback shows in Paris after her incurable diagnosis — a defiant act of artistry that resonates far beyond the stage.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Interior scene inside a warm clinical examination room at Mass General Brigham's Heart and Vascular Institute, looking outward through a tall window toward golden afternoon Boston skyline, rich amber sunlight streaming through the glass and casting long warm rectangles across gleaming white linoleum and honey-toned wood paneling. The room glows with saturated gold and vibrant emerald — sunlight refracting through a potted plant on the windowsill, throwing leaf-shadow patterns across the walls. In the foreground a research nurse in a white coat gently holds a sleek pale medical auto-injector pen, caught in a reverent shaft of afternoon gold, the device gleaming like something quietly precious. A middle-aged patient sits calmly in a clinical chair, trusting and still. On a desk nearby a tablet screen displays a cholesterol panel with two stark numbers in dramatic contrast, and beside it a cross-section diagram of a clean open artery glowing pink and unobstructed, smooth-walled, a road cleared before any accident. A subtle calendar on the wall shows gentle marks every fourteen days across five years of patient persistence. Through the window the outside world is warm and alive with saturated emerald treetops catching full afternoon sun, the feeling of something long-awaited finally confirmed, quiet triumph filling every gold-drenched corner of the room.
